name: Verb Focus and Aspect (mag- and um-)
nameNative: Pokus at aspekto ng pandiwa (mag- at um-)
summary: Understand actor-focus verbs with mag- and um-, and the three aspects: completed, ongoing, and not-yet-begun.
explanation:
Filipino verbs do not have tense the way English does. Instead they have aspect: whether an action is completed, ongoing, or not yet started. Two common actor-focus affixes are mag- and um-, which both put the focus on the doer of the action.

Take the root kain (eat), which uses the um- affix. With um- the root takes an infix: kumain (ate, completed), kumakain (is eating, ongoing), kakain (will eat, not yet begun). With mag-, the root sulat (write) gives nagsulat (wrote), nagsusulat (is writing), magsusulat (will write).

The ongoing and not-yet forms reduplicate the first syllable of the root (ka-kain, su-sulat). Choosing mag- versus um- depends on the specific verb, and many verbs only take one. Learn each common verb with its affix as a set, the way you would learn an irregular English verb.
pattern: um-: -um- infix + reduplication | mag-: nag- / nagCV- / magCV-
commonMistakes: Looking for a tense. Filipino marks aspect (completed/ongoing/not-yet), not past/present/future.
Forgetting to reduplicate the first syllable for ongoing and not-yet forms.
Mixing mag- and um- on the same root randomly. Each verb prefers one; learn them as sets.
Using the infinitive form alone to mean past tense. The completed form is needed.
Putting nag- on an um- verb, producing forms like nagkain instead of kumain.
tip: Reduplication is your time signal: a doubled first syllable means the action is ongoing or still to come.
